perturbation.original_text: (d) Governing Law . This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of Singapore and shall be subject to the non -exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Singapore.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) Governing Law . This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of Singapore.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause stating that the agreement is subject to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Singapore could be problematic if a dispute arises and one party wishes to pursue action in another jurisdiction where they may have a stronger legal position or assets. This could hinder a party's ability to seek legal recourse effectively.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore courts have jurisdiction over contracts performed in Singapore. This modification could imply other jurisdictions are appropriate, creating ambiguity that could violate fundamental principles of contract law regarding jurisdiction.
perturbation.law_citation: Singapore Civil Law Act, sections related to contract law and jurisdiction (Specific sections vary based on case law interpretation, but generally principles of contract law are codified and interpreted with regard to jurisdiction)
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the specific reference to the Singapore court's jurisdiction, the contract creates ambiguity about where disputes should be resolved. Singapore contract law, interpreted by Singapore courts, presumes jurisdiction within Singapore for contracts performed there. The changed text contradicts this presumption by omission, potentially violating principles of legal certainty and predictability in contract enforcement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Ch. 08 The Law of Contract www.singaporelawwatch.sg COMMERCIAL LAW Search Ch. 08 The Law of Contract Ch. 08A Remedies for Breach of Contract Ch. 09 Domestic Sale of Goods Ch. 10 International Sale of Goods Ch. 11 The Law of Credit and Security Ch. 12 Intellectual Property Law Ch. 13 Intellectual Property Licensing Ch. 14 Forms of Business Organisations Ch. 15 Law of Agency Ch. 16 Singapore Company Law Ch. 17 Corporate Finance and Securities Regulation Ch. 18 Equity and Trusts Ch. 19 Restitution Ch. 20 The Law of Negligence Ch. 21 Economic Torts Ch. 22 Banking and Finance Ch. 23 The Law of Guarantees Ch. 24 Insurance Law Ch. 25 Shipping Law Ch. 26 Building and Construction Law Ch. 27 Competition Law Ch. 28 Income Taxation Ch. 29 Land Law Ch. 30 Bankruptcy and Insolvency Ch. 31 Technology, Media, and Telecom No content A problem occurred while loading content. Previous Next Ch. 08 The Law of Contract SECTION 1 GENERAL APPLICATION A. Singapore contract law largely based on English contract law 8.1.1 Contract law in Singapore is largely based on the common law of contract in England. Hence, the rules developed in the Singapore courts do bear a very close resemblance to those developed under English common law. Indeed, where there is no Singapore authority specifically on point, it will usually be assumed that the position will, in the first instance, be no different from that in England. Unlike its neighbours Malaysia and Brunei, following Independence in 1965, Singapore’s Parliament made no attempt to codify Singapore’s law of contract. Accordingly, much of the law of contract in Singapore remains in the form of judge-made rules. B. Certain aspects of Singapore contract law based on specific statutes 8.1.2 In some circumstances, these judge-made rules have been modified by specific statutes. Many of these statutes are English in origin. To begin with, 13 English commercial statutes have been incorporated as part of the Statutes of the Republic of Singapore by virtue of s 4 of the Application of English Law Act (Cap 7A, 1993 Rev Ed). These are listed in Part II of the First Schedule of this Act. Other statutes, eg the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act (Cap 53B, 2002 Rev Ed), are modelled upon English statutes. There are also other areas where statutory development based on non-English models has taken place, eg the Consumer Protection (Fair Trading) Act (Cap 52A, 2004 Rev Ed) (which was largely drawn from fair trading legislation enacted in Alberta and Sasketchewan). SECTION 2 OFFER AND ACCEPTANCE A. Contract is essentially agreement between two or more parties 8.2.1 A contract is essentially an agreement between two or more parties, the terms of which affect their respective rights and obligations which are enforceable at law. Whether the parties have reached agreement, or a meeting of the minds, is objectively ascertained from the facts. The concepts of offer and acceptance provide in many, albeit not all, cases the starting point for analysing whether agreement has been reached. B. Offer 1. Definition of offer 8.2.2 An offer is a promise, or other expression of willingness, by the ‘offeror’ to be bound on certain specified terms upon the unqualified acceptance of these terms by the person to whom the offer is made (the ‘offeree’). Provided the other formation elements (ie consideration and intention to create legal relations) are present, the acceptance of an offer results in a valid contract. 2. Offer must be made with objectively ascertained intention to be bound 8.2.3 Whether any particular statement amounts to an offer depends on the intention with which it is made. An offer must be made with the intention to be bound. On the other hand, if a person is merely soliciting offers or requesting for information, without any intention to be bound, at best, he or she would be making an invitation to treat. Under the objective test, a person may be said to have made an offer if his or her statement (or conduct) induces a reas
perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Singapore' clause creates a legal contradiction. The law explanation states Singapore contract law presumes jurisdiction within Singapore for contracts performed there. By removing the jurisdiction clause, the changed text contradicts this presumption, potentially violating principles of legal certainty, which are implied under Singapore contract law as described in Scraped Law Snippet 2 referencing the 'Law of Contract' in Singapore. The removal allows ambiguity, undermining the enforceability of the contract within Singapore's legal framework.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall exercise reasonable care to separate all Confidential Information and all information generated by the Receiving Party based thereon from all documents and other records of the Receiving Party , and shall otherwise exercise reasonable care to prevent the unauthorized use or discl osure of the Disclosing Party ’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall exercise care to prevent the unauthorized use or disclosure of the Disclosing Party’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'reasonable' care and removing the clause about separating information means they are not obligated to keep information separate from other documents, decreasing the protection on the data and increasing the risk of it being leaked. This contradicts standards for data protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 24 of the PDPA requires organizations to protect personal data in their possession or under their control by making reasonable security arrangements to prevent unauthorized access, collection, use, disclosure, copying, modification, disposal or similar risks. By removing the 'reasonable care' and information separation requirements, the modified text reduces the standard of data protection below what is considered reasonable under the PDPA, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the 'reasonable care' standard and the requirement to separate confidential information. The Law Explanation states that Section 24 of the PDPA requires organizations to protect personal data by making reasonable security arrangements to prevent unauthorized access or disclosure. The omission of 'reasonable care' weakens the data protection standard below what is considered reasonable under the PDPA, creating a contradiction. Although the provided scraped snippets are not directly helpful in detailing Section 24, the Law Explanation provides a strong basis for the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party (including its Affiliates and Representatives) do not acquire any rights, express or implied, in the Confidential Information of Disclosing Party (including its Affiliates), except for the limited use specified in this Agreement. The Confidential Information of Disclosing Party (and its Affiliates), including all right, title and interest therein, remain the sole and exclusive property of Disclosing Party (and its Affiliates).
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party (including its Affiliates and Representatives) do not acquire any rights, express or implied, in the Confidential Information of Disclosing Party (including its Affiliates), except for the limited use specified in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the sentence explicitly stating that the Confidential Information remains the sole and exclusive property of the Disclosing Party could lead to disputes over ownership and intellectual property rights. This omission introduces ambiguity that can contradict established legal principles protecting intellectual property.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Singapore Copyright Act, Trademarks Act, and Trade Secrets Law (common law principles)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 19 of the Singapore Copyright Act specifies ownership of copyright. Although this NDA concerns 'Confidential Information' which may or may not be copyrightable, the principle of ownership is fundamental. By omitting the clear statement of ownership, the contract creates a loophole where the Receiving Party could argue for implied rights or ownership, contradicting the intended protection of the Disclosing Party's intellectual property and creating legal risk under Singapore law.
perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly stated that the Disclosing Party retains sole ownership of the Confidential Information. The changed text omits this critical sentence, weakening the Disclosing Party's protection of their intellectual property. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this omission creates ambiguity that could lead to disputes over ownership. The Law Explanation suggests that by omitting the clear statement of ownership, the Receiving Party could argue for implied rights. This aligns with the 'Omission Legal Contradiction' definition because the contract removes essential information relating to legally mandated consumer protection, making it potentially non-compliant.
